speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking with Mrs. Sonia Zyroff. I am Marsha [? Blitstein. ?] This is Bloomingdale, Illinois, USA. And this is the third tape. Mrs. Zyroff you were telling us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so to your question. You asked that the farmer told us to get out because the Jews smells, right? Yeah. So we, the four of us, started working to a man my brother knew to a different name of a village, different name. [NON-ENGLISH], it was called. A different village to come to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had whatever we had with us, you know, not much. So my brother said to me, you-- you sit here. There was a barn there and the-- the-- the house was further away. So I was sitting under the barn, outside the barn. And they were gonna knock at the window there and ask for bread so-- and come back. We came and I'm sitting and waiting and waiting and waiting. They never come back. Daylight starts to come. What's happened to my brother, my sister, and that girl? I'm the only one sitting in there. So I tried to get in the barn. If there is a God with miracles, this was the time. I walk in the barn and I walk over bodies. Bodies. Who were these bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazis. All young Nazis. And the farmer my-- my brother went to was a big Nazi, but he was a friend of my brother. So when my brother-- my sister-- my brother knocked at the door-- later I find out-- at the window, that friend of my brother opened up the window and told them, run. Run, because we have all the Nazis here sleeping in the barn and in the-- in the house full. Run. Because that was 1943. At that time, the war was between the Ukrainian and the Polacks. So the Ukrainian was organizing the Nazis. The Jews were already all killed. But the Ukrainian was fighting the Polack. And the public Pollack was fighting back the Ukrainians. So during the night, the Ukrainian bandits were getting together, having axes and knives and attacking Polish colonies. So one time, the-- the man-- that man told me-- I'll tell you later how he-- told me a story that they-- they-- that he was the Nazi leader when they attacked a Polish colony. He saw what his colleagues did, the young Nazi bandits. There was a Polish pregnant girl. They cut her stomach open, took the baby out, and let the Polish girl die like that. You-- in the daytime, the-- the Pollacks got united with the Germans, and they'll attack colonies where they suspected these bandits and surround them during the night and also kill-- kill-- kill the people and put the farm on-- the farm on fire. They put-- That was 1943. It was burning day and night. In a way, I was happy to see it. That now they're killing each other. The Jews are all gone. There's no problem with the Jews. Now they're killing each other. But the problem was I was hiding in-- in-- in barns. So now I have to continue here the story. So when he-- my brother-- when the guy told my brother to run, they couldn't come back to the barn where I was sitting because full of Nazis were laying in the barn, and I was walking over them and not one woke up to catch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when start of daylight, I took whatever I had. And I-- was a straw roof for the pigs-- pigs-- where the pigs were living. And then I got into the pig barn. And then from the pigs, I crawled somewhere and I went on top of the attic, the same farm, to sit there, because I had no choice. If they're gonna kill me, they're going to kill me whatever it's gonna

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I showed myself up for the guy. He came into the barn. And I told him. He was-- he was a Nazi, but knowing my brother, he brought me something to eat. And sit a while in his barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the Nazi leaders were not afraid. To them, nobody was going to come look for a Jew, you know what I mean? So-- so anyway, he brought me something to eat. The same day in the afternoon, he brings that girl that was with my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and my sister ran away. That girl couldn't keep up with them. She left behind. She was all full of blood so the farmer went to his farm. He found that girl. She had a piece of glass. She was cutting her wrist open. She wanted to die because she was left there laying in the fields. And she was all full of blood and was still on the same farmer's farm. And he picked her up and cleaned up her hand and brought her upstairs to sit with me. So we sat there for quite a-- quite a while. But see, I forgot to tell you. In the beginning, right in the beginning, right after I washed myself up from the toilet, I went to a friend called [? Manka. ?] This is where I have on the film here. In the film. She's there. And with the pictures. These are the people we went back to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her father was a Nazi leader. I didn't know that. So she was not afraid. She kept me in the house for few weeks, maybe longer. Maybe-- I don't remember. Sitting on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had the barrel, would make bread in front of me. And I was sitting in the back on top of-- of an oven. And evidently, her father knew whatever. So she kept me a while. And the Hitler picture was hanging right big in front of the dining room table. So I knew that he was-- was a Nazi leader. And I stayed there. It was a Christmas Eve one night. And then when they would-- I-- they didn't go to me when he was home. She brought me to eat only when he was gone. So-- but I didn't question it. So he was home and then they're eating the Christmas dinner. And then I see the Hitler picture is rolling, rolling, rolling, rolling, and fell right down on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say-- and then the next day, that [? Manka ?] comes up to me. She says, you know what that means? That means that Hitler is gonna go down and you're still gonna be alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the father was a Nazi leader. He was killed. He never lived to see the door-- the war. Never the end of the was. He a big there. When they captured my parents for hiding 15 months, they brought it back to him. He was the officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Safka ?] [INAUDIBLE]. [? Safka ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do know how to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But his daughter was hiding me. His wife knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were hiding me. So [? Safka ?] feel-- felt bad, they told me now, that my parents were captured. But he did my parents a big favor. The guy who captured them was gonna drive-- he was gonna ride the horses and drive my parents by [? wood ?] to the-- running by foot to the city to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did my parents a favor, [? Safka. ?] He ordered the horse and buggy they should take him to the cemetery where they're supposed to be killed. They told me that now when I was there. It was two years ago. That [? Safka ?] was-- felt bad that my parents were captured.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, that was his daughter that was hiding me. See, so they brought them there to-- to be killed. So they did the favor. So that was the story from-- from before. Now we'll go back to me sitting at that Nazis area. It was a good place away from the city. But the war with the Ukrainians. The Ukrainian burning, burning day and night. The whole Ukrainian, by the end of '43, was black.

speaker: interviewee
text: So many killed. So many burned. And they were killing each other. One-- the one of the Ukrainians who-- who was telling my sister who the girl captured to-- I'm going back. He told my sister the plans that that lady made. He was one of the four so to kill us and bury us on the farm. He was one of them to capture us. So then when she let my sister go, he told her her plans, that she had their graves ready on the farm to capture us both and kill us. When I went back at night, remember this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So the-- the-- this guy killed his brother to take his boots away. So this is how they were going killing each other, the Ukrainians, like-- like-- like animals. So here I'm on the roof sitting there for a month or so, or two. I don't know. They were feeding the two of us. And one morning in the summer time-- it was September, the month of September-- the girl who was with me wakes me up. Wake up, she says. There is something going on here. I hear noises. I look out through the little peek hole and they are surrounded-- the farm is surrounded by the Nazis from the city, by I don't know who it was. But surrounded because they found out that here, the Nazis have the hiding place. They didn't mean to look for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what do I do? They're gonna search. So the barn roof, you know how high it is? And I'm sitting on the roof. I jump off the roof from the back. This girl jumps with me, and we run.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are on horses running after us right away. And there was a-- a creek of water. With the horses, they were afraid to go into that creek, the horses might sink. They had to go around the bridge. But the meantime, I was running up the hill, and they were shooting after me. The girl, they captured. She couldn't run as fast as I could. I was a big runner. So they were shooting after me. Haystacks already. It was September. Haystacks sending with harvest. And a bullet went in the haystacks and made a smoke and fire. And one bullet went through my hair. And I ran. And that's already a different, a lot of fields. And it's a different village, a different name. There used to be a hospital they had burned down. I go in the hospital and I sit a minute. And I thought, no. I made up my mind they're not going to get me alive. If I crawl, if I sit here, they'll get me alive. I walk out of that basement. I go and sit in the bushes, right maybe two feet away from the bushes. Bushes high. Nobody was cutting grass. It was a war. And I'm sitting in those bushes. Five minutes later, a boy brings them and says, here she went in. I heard him say it because I was sitting right there. She went in. They searched that basement. They didn't find me. And me sitting in the bushes outside. They sat on the horses. It was already closer to the evening. And they went back to the city. And then evening started-- started coming. And it's cold and I'm all wet. I went through the water. Through-- I thought, I drown, I'll drown. But I'll go through the water no matter what. And then it started getting dark. I'm going back to the farm because on the attic, I left a little jacket, a bottle of water, and a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have to go back. So I come back. New people are taking care of the farmer's animals. The one who were there before got arrested and they took him away. And they-- some new, some new Gentile people are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called the Nazis to let them know. As soon as they saw me walking in the barn, they came and put a great big lock on the door and they called the Nazis to come and get me. But that farmer who was hiding us for a while made specially a couple boards loose in the back of the barn and said if anything sometimes comes to the front, you get out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it got dark. I took my bundles and I walked out. They searched. They came a little later. They searched. They couldn't find me and I went. That time, I walked almost a whole night until I got to the place where I went to see the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with them at the beginning a month, that [? Manka ?] that we went to see now. And I crawled in-- in the barn during the night. And I'm sitting there and I thought, I'm all alone now but this is the end. Because I knew the father's a Nazi. I knew whatever they're gonna do with me, I'm all alone. I have nothing to live for. They kill me. They'll kill me. So I sit there for three days. What I am eating, there was straw, straw from the harvesting. There was little grains of food to nibble out. So-- in the barns, we lived with the spiders. We made feasts with spiders and rats and all of that. Now if I see a spider, I'm screaming my head off. At that time, I was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, three days, I am sitting and starving. I thought, well-- [? Manka's ?] husband, [INAUDIBLE]-- I'll mention him later-- comes in the barn, takes the farming fork to take something for the animals to feed, and I am up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, [NON-ENGLISH], and I show myself up. [INAUDIBLE] takes a look at me. He drops the fork and he faints and falls on the floor. Fainted. He went back to the house. So now I am waiting. Whatever is gonna be, it's gonna be. Maybe a half hour later, his wife-- the one who was hiding me in the house a month before-- so she was a friend of mine and the father was a Nazi.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she comes with a glass of hot milk, a pitcher like of hot milk, and some pirogi she baked. It was a Sunday morning. Comes with this and crawls right up there and sits and cries with me. And from that time, they kept me to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian soldiers found me on the attic down there. It's a very interesting story. I don't know how busy you are, but that, I have to tell you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we want to know how you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how I was-- how I was--

words.text: how I was-- how I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to know that experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: --liberated. That's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now you ask me, ask me-- you know, whatever I tell you, it's just a 10th of the experience. From-- from-- it-- it's just so-- so much torture. And so I was so big as nothing. But then the last few months when I stayed at [? Manka's, ?] she had a sister, [? Anya. ?] She's in the picture. She was 17 years old. She was the one to bring the food to the barn, sitting with me day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Anya ?] or [? Manka? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anya. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Anya ?] was the one--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Manka ?] had [? Sonia, ?] little girl. She was seven years old going to school, and kept it a secret. I sawed her-- she remembered now, she told me, she remembered that she wore a dress that I sewed for her by hand, that she loved it but her mother wouldn't tell her where she got the fabric.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she get the fabric? When they were killing the Jews. That's one thing I want you to know. You'll never know from anybody else. The Jews had to undress themselves, completely nothing, before they lie in the grave. Put the clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they made announcement. Anybody who wants to come to get Jewish clothes. First, they search them if there's anything hidden, and then come get the clothes. So [? Manka ?] got a lot of these Jewish clothes. But they wouldn't wear them the way they were. The size wasn't good or whatever. So while I was sitting there, I had some work to do. So evidently, at least I paid for them my food or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was a good friend besides because in the beginning, the biggest danger was they scared all the other nationalities. If you give a Jew a piece of bread, you go in the same grave with the Jew. See? So but she was hiding me, and her father a Nazi. So she was not as much afraid. But he knew. Didn't say anything. Now, did she have a husband that was aware of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's true. OK, so it was this couple--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was her husband--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and she had the daughter, the daughter [? Sonia. ?] And then now when we went, she had another daughter. [? Tania ?] was born after the war, with the husband and two daughters. And we stayed with them the whole couple of weeks at their house. And my son had such a good time with them. They were so nice. They were treating us royally. And my son brought them a big bundle. He gave them a lot of money. Gave [? Manka ?] money. And [? Sonia ?] got money, and [INAUDIBLE] got money. They all-- we gave them whatever-- whatever we could, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the last few months. So let's get there some-- some stories here in between. Ask me. Well, I-- when you were liberated, when-- when you were found, what-- what happened at that point? What turns did your life take?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. But let me tell you how I was found first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm sitting there. Nazis are looking for me. They all know that I am alive someplace because they knew I had run away. They stopped searching in that particular village. They were searching in the other village. In the daytime, they saw me running, shooting after me. There was a different village called [INAUDIBLE]. So they were looking for me there. They stopped looking where I really was, came back to-- to [? Manka's. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I am at Manka's and I work. And sometimes, it was a holiday, they put the blankets on the windows, and they take me down once-- once to eat with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One night-- so when I used to hear the German language, I was dying. That's one point I don't want to forget. So when the Germans were around looking for me, this [INAUDIBLE], [? Manka's ?] husband, dug a hole in the barn [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and put me down, put a barrel board on top, and straw on top, and the horses over me. When the horses peed, it was going right all over me. But that doesn't count. I want to live. So he-- he was telling that on the tape also, how when he knew they-- they still looking for me, so he was hiding me right in the barn, and the horses were staying on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, after they left, I [? crawl ?] out and sit back. So I was sitting back in the-- in that barn on the rooftop. You know. I cut out a little-- like a little window for daylight, because I did some sewing by hand. One middle of the night, March '44, I wake up, and I hear the Russian language. Russian language? I thought, God is speaking to me. I'm dreaming. It is not true.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it bothers me sometimes when American Jews hate Communism. Their-- their belief don't mean nothing to me, but they were not Jew killers. They were-- anyway, they speaking Russian. And before I know, there were six soldiers. I heard the lieutenant say that he sees a little small window on top over there, and it's gonna be wonderful for him to look on because the Germans were digging in around Horochow. Just digging in. They took over the city and digging in. The war was going on still three more months. Digging in right around the city. And we are just a few miles away, and that farm is very high up. And they could see what the Germans are doing. Before I know, the lieutenant crawls right on top of me. Lucky, I know the language. The gun out. He wants to shoot. Says, in-- in Russian I have to explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: That guy sat all day long listening, to believe. They couldn't believe why would they want. Why? He grabs a gun and he wants to kill [? Manka ?] and [INAUDIBLE]. It's winter. Keeping a human being outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to beg him, don't do it. They saving my life. They didn't understand. Well anyway, he sat all day long listening to me to tell them what's what. I was sitting that last eight months at hers. My legs stopped functioning. I didn't walk. My hearing, I only whispered. All the years, just whispering. And then-- so I could-- when I-- tried to talk, I was deaf. I couldn't see what I'm talking. But anyway, I tried to tell the guy who I am, what I am. Sat all day. And then for the night, they want to go around. They're spies, you know. And they want to leave me there. I started to cry. I'm going with you. He says, you're going to get killed. I says, I'm gonna get killed. I'd rather be killed that way. I had nothing to live for. I want to be killed. It's against the Russian law. We are spies. We're not supposed to have you. I says, put me in as another spy. I am going with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me down to the house, to [? Manka. ?] [? Manka ?] gave us something to eat. Tried to give me a pair of shoes or whatever to wear. I went for the night with the soldiers crawling around all under the German front lines, all on the people that living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We happened to go to that place that I knew they were hiding us before, where I jumped off the roof. They were all on that borderline. We crawled around all night long. And then we went back to a suburb of Horochow called [INAUDIBLE]. All the civilians were evacuated because the front line was establishing right in that place. And lucky for me that I left with those soldiers. I would-- they evacuated the rest of the people, and I could have died in that hole, sitting there by myself, because they couldn't take me with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told me that-- that now. And we knew what happened. So we come into a city, to a-- to a house, and they want to have breakfast. Everybody-- the people left the cows and they left the chickens. And they went and got eggs and they had-- got milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're sitting and we're eating and the ceiling falls down, the windows fall down. They look out the window. The German is in front of the house, and we are on the back of the house. And we started running. Those six soldiers took the orders of the lieutenant. I didn't weigh much, but they dragged me like a dead animal. I was sitting there, I was writing my life history. And I lost everything there when they were dragging and running with me. I had bundles, you know, saved, but I was trying to write something down every day. We ran like that 15 kilometers. Hand grenades and-- and-- and whatever were falling after us, after the Russian soldiers. And they knew their strategic point was next to a river where they're swamps only. And the hand grenades were falling in the swamp, only the mud went up in the air. One of the soldiers got some-- wounded. One of them had his coat full of holes. And we ran like that 15 kilometers where the Russian front line was established. So then I lay all night with all the soldiers on the ground. And nobody even looked because I looked like-- you can picture me, how I looked like. In the morning, they had to report they found somebody. To bring me to the Russian headquarters to report. Well, at that time I'm already living. You know. And the Russian-- nobody was going to kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they bring me into the main office of the big leaders of the war for questioning. So they spread out a map and they trying to find out where I'm coming from. And I told them, and they can't find it. So what I do is take my finger while I'm standing there and show them, here is. They looked at me. Arrest her. She's a spy. That creature knows to read a map? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm arrested. Two soldiers with the guns up in the air had orders not to talk to me. Orders not to talk to me, and they take me someplace. I don't know where or what. I'm a spy. While we are walking, I'm barefooted in the snow, in the mud, wearing a jacket with a straw belt to hold it together. A creature to look. I start begging these two soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please. Only one answer. I want from you one word. Tell me are there any Jews in the Russian army? They talked it over in themselves, and they answered, my lieutenant is a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The general, the-- the biggest leader, the major general or whatever, is a Jew. And we have right here several Jews right in this village. And the first one [INAUDIBLE]. I says, just do me one thing. Just let a Jew know that I am looking for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they pick up there. And I talk to the people, whatever. I don't know what they said. And they decided to take me to a place where all the officers, they occupied the Czechoslovakian area with houses, the first quarters of the Russian military. They had a kitchen there to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bring me to that dining room. And I'm sitting there and waiting. The officers come to eat. This creature is sitting and looking. And I see one lieutenant, so attractive and so dressed to kill, take a cross-eye look, went to eat first. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After he got to eat, he comes back. And he said, you want to see me? I says, yes. He was from-- from Kiev. Later, I met somebody from-- from Leningrad. I am from Kiev. I am Jewish. Tell me. I know something about your story. What can I do for you? Give you money? I don't want any money. I don't want to be arrested, number one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't worry about that. You're not arrested. You need help. And I'm not the only one. There's-- it's a Captain [INAUDIBLE], and there's Major General so-and-so that's a Jew. They let each other know that that funny creature is there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next morning, they come with a Jeep like picking me up and taking me to the military headquarters to dress me up. They put on a pair of military boots on me. I can hardly lift them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they gave me the thick wool blankets and they gave me a lot of linings. They had all of that. So by hand [? there and the ?] Czech, they put me up in a Czech family where they were eating. And I start sewing dress, make myself some shoes out of the fabric and-- and do everything. And then wash myself up and put me to sleep. I had to sleep on the floor because only when the military was, they were watched for Nazis. Because it was still full of Nazis all the way around. But the Russians were on the guard already, so I wasn't worried.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I stayed there over a month with them. Every night, all these generals and lieutenants had to get together. Hello, Sonia. Let's have a l'chaim, [RUSSIAN], drink by the glass of the vodka.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so skinny. To build me up and bring-- and bring me some food. Well, I sort of was thankful to God I'm alive. I-- I just didn't know what miracle happened to me that these people so interesting. So it was Captain [INAUDIBLE] from Leningrad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll send you back to my wife and children and the family. A month later, they-- I didn't want to go. Where do you want to go? I had family in America. Maybe I can go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but anyway, a month later, they have to go on Berlin. They started to say goodbye to me. And they cried. I cried. Because they went on Berlin, and we don't know if they survived or not to go home to their families. But that month, they really built me up and make a-- made a mensch out of me. But before they left--
